preamble.summary: The king's sleepless night and Haman's humiliation. The LXX follows the MT closely in this chapter. The pivotal irony — Haman advising his own public honor for Mordecai — is preserved in both versions. The LXX adds that God caused the king's sleeplessness.
preamble.notable_variants: The LXX attributes the king's insomnia to divine action: 'the Lord removed sleep from the king.' The MT simply states that sleep fled the king. This is the most significant theological addition in this chapter.

verses.lxx_reading: ὁ δὲ κύριος ἀπέστησεν τὸν ὕπνον ἀπὸ τοῦ βασιλέως
verses.variant_rendering: the Lord removed sleep from the king
variant_notes: The MT's masterful ambiguity — is the insomnia coincidence or providence? — is resolved by the LXX which explicitly names 'the Lord' (ho kyrios) as the agent. This is characteristic of the LXX Esther's theological program: making explicit what the MT leaves implicit.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.

verses.lxx_reading: τί ποιήσω τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ ὃν ἐγὼ θέλω δοξάσαι
verses.variant_rendering: what shall I do for the man whom I wish to glorify?
verses.mt_rendering: what shall be done for the man whom the king wishes to honor?
variant_notes: The LXX has the king speak in first person ('whom I wish'), while the MT uses third person ('whom the king wishes'). Both versions set up the devastating irony.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.

verses.lxx_reading: οἱ φίλοι αὐτοῦ καὶ Ζωσαρα ἡ γυνὴ αὐτοῦ
verses.variant_rendering: his friends and Zosara his wife
verses.mt_rendering: his wise men and Zeresh his wife
variant_notes: The LXX renders Zeresh as Zosara and changes 'wise men' to 'friends.' The ominous warning — 'if Mordecai is of Jewish descent, you will surely fall' — is preserved in both versions.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.
